name: Skill Tree
description: A graph or branching structure where players spend progression resources to unlock abilities, passives, or build modifiers.
design_notes: Skill trees are planning interfaces, prerequisite graphs, and spend transactions at the same time. The graph needs stable node IDs, prerequisite_node_ids, visible costs, respec rules, migration-safe save data, and soft-lock prevention when content changes.
design_purpose: Let players plan character growth across sessions or runs.
Create build identity through mutually reinforcing nodes and prerequisites.
Pace ability unlocks so new verbs and passive bonuses arrive at understandable intervals.
player_fantasy: The player feels they are shaping a build through intentional long-term choices.

parameters.name: node_id
parameters.description: Stable identifier for a purchasable or granted node in the tree.

parameters.name: prerequisite_node_ids
parameters.description: Node IDs required before a target node can unlock.

parameters.name: skill_point_cost
parameters.description: Spendable progression cost required to purchase the node.

parameters.name: respec_refund_ratio
parameters.description: Share of spent resources returned when the node is refunded.

parameters.name: branch_depth_limit
parameters.description: Maximum path depth used to pace powerful unlocks and validate layout.

edge_cases: Removing a prerequisite node during respec must also remove or disable dependent nodes.
Content patches that move or rename node_id values need migration rules for existing saves.
UI navigation must handle large trees with keyboard, mouse, gamepad, touch, zoom, and search.
Mutually exclusive branches require clear warnings before irreversible choices.
A prerequisite cycle or disconnected root can soft-lock progression if graph validation does not run before release.
common_bugs: A node can be purchased without prerequisites because UI and backend validation differ.
Refunding a node leaves granted abilities, passive stats, or hotbar actions active.
Circular prerequisites lock the tree or make validation recurse forever.
Localization changes node labels but not search, filters, or controller focus order.
Save data stores node array indexes instead of node_id and breaks after content reordering.
balancing_notes: Avoid mandatory tax nodes that every build must buy unless they serve a clear tutorial purpose.
Powerful synergies need opportunity cost, branch distance, class restriction, or diminishing returns.
Validate that every build path has enough spendable points to reach at least one satisfying identity before the curve ends.
accessibility_notes: Support zoom, search, readable focus order, and text scaling for large trees.
Use icons plus text labels so node meaning is not carried only by color or shape.

implementation_notes.unity: Store nodes as ScriptableObjects or JSON keyed by node_id, validate prerequisite graph connectivity in editor tests, and commit purchases through a skill_point spend transaction before applying ability grants.
implementation_notes.godot: Represent nodes as Resources with prerequisite_node_ids and run validation before exporting; keep visual graph layout separate from unlock rules and saved purchased node IDs.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use Primary Data Assets or DataTables for nodes and centralize purchase validation in a component or subsystem that handles skill_point_cost, respec refunds, and save migration.
implementation_notes.web: Load graph data as JSON, compute unlock eligibility in pure functions, and keep layout metadata separate from progression state so URL tools and editors can inspect node_id graphs.

relationships.reason: Skill trees commonly spend earned points or levels as the currency for node unlocks.
Respec rules make skill-tree experimentation safer by allowing controlled rollback of unlocked nodes.
Dense skill graphs and inventory grids can compete for menu complexity and controller focus patterns.

example_games.title: Path of Exile
example_games.note: Uses a large passive tree for build planning.

example_games.title: The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt
example_games.note: Uses skill investment menus to shape Geralt's combat and utility abilities.

example_games.title: Diablo II
example_games.note: Uses class skill investment to define play style.

example_games.title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim
example_games.note: Uses perk trees tied to skill progression and build choices.
